name: Perceived Usefulness
definition: The degree to which a person believes that using a particular system would enhance his or her job performance.
sourcePaper: Davis (1989) - Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and User Acceptance of Information Technology
measurementItems: Using the system improves my performance in my job.
Using the system increases my productivity.
Using the system enhances my effectiveness.
I find the system useful in my job.
notes: Core construct in technology acceptance. Often mediates the effect of PEOU on intention.

name: Perceived Ease of Use
definition: The degree to which a person believes that using a particular system would be free of effort.
sourcePaper: Davis (1989) - Perceived Usefulness, Perceived Ease of Use, and User Acceptance of Information Technology
measurementItems: Learning to operate the system is easy for me.
I find it easy to get the system to do what I want.
My interaction with the system is clear and understandable.
I find the system flexible to interact with.
notes: Often has both direct and indirect (via PU) effects on behavioral intention.

name: Behavioral Intention
definition: A person's subjective probability that he or she will perform a specified behavior.
sourcePaper: Fishbein & Ajzen (1975) - Belief, Attitude, Intention, and Behavior
theoreticalDomain: Theory of Reasoned Action / Planned Behavior
measurementItems: I intend to use the system in the future.
I will try to use the system in my daily work.
I plan to continue using the system.
notes: Key predictor of actual behavior in TRA/TPB. Mediates attitudes toward behavior.

name: Confirmation
definition: The extent to which the user's experience with the information system confirms his or her pre-acceptance expectations.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - Understanding Information Systems Continuance
measurementItems: My experience with using the system was better than I expected.
The service level provided by the system was better than I expected.
Overall, most of my expectations from using the system were confirmed.
notes: Central to post-adoption research. Disconfirmation (negative confirmation) drives satisfaction changes.

name: Satisfaction
definition: The affective state reflecting the degree to which the user is pleased with the prior use of the information system.
sourcePaper: Oliver (1980) - A Cognitive Model of the Antecedents and Consequences of Satisfaction
measurementItems: I am satisfied with the performance of the system.
I am pleased with the experience of using the system.
I am contented with the use of the system.
notes: Key mediator between confirmation and continuance intention in ECM.

name: Continuance Intention
definition: The intention of a user to continue using an information system following initial adoption.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - Understanding Information Systems Continuance
measurementItems: I intend to continue using the system rather than discontinue.
My intentions are to continue using the system.
I will keep using the system as regularly as I do now.
notes: Post-adoption equivalent of behavioral intention. Different from adoption intention.

name: Expectations
definition: Pre-consumption beliefs about product or service performance.
sourcePaper: Oliver (1980) - A Cognitive Model of the Antecedents and Consequences of Satisfaction
measurementItems: Pre-exposure expectation items adapted to context
notes: Forms baseline for confirmation/disconfirmation.

name: Perceived Performance
definition: The user's perception of how the system actually performed.
sourcePaper: Oliver (1980) - Expectation-Confirmation Theory
measurementItems: Performance perception items adapted to IS context
notes: Compared against expectations to form confirmation.

name: Actual Usage
definition: The actual behavior of using the information system.
sourcePaper: Davis (1989) - TAM
measurementItems: Self-reported or system-captured usage metrics
notes: Outcome of behavioral intention in adoption models.

name: Prior Expectations
definition: What users believed the technology would do for them before they started using it. A cognitive pre-use state that becomes the benchmark against which actual experience is judged.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - ECM-IS; Oliver (1980) - ECT
measurementItems: Before using this product, I expected it to improve my farming decisions.
notes: In agritech, a farmer might expect a weather app to help them decide when to plant.

name: Continued Use
definition: The actual sustained usage of the technology over time. Often proxied by Continuance Intention in studies; can be measured via usage logs or follow-up surveys.
sourcePaper: Bhattacherjee (2001) - ECM-IS
measurementItems: Longitudinal usage data
Follow-up behavioural measures
notes: In Sub-Saharan Africa, gap between intention and behaviour may be influenced by infrastructural barriers (connectivity, cost, literacy).

name: Task Characteristics
definition: The attributes and requirements of the tasks that users need to perform. Includes task complexity, variety, interdependence, and the information and processing demands the task places on the user.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Task complexity, variety, and interdependence items adapted to context
notes: One of two antecedents of fit; describes what the task demands from the user and the technology.

name: Technology Characteristics
definition: The capabilities, features, and constraints of the technology. What the system can do, how it presents information, and how well it supports the user's work.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Technology capability and functionality items adapted to context
notes: One of two antecedents of fit; describes what the technology provides.

name: Task-Technology Fit
definition: The degree to which technology functionality matches the requirements of the tasks the user performs. The fit itself is measurable: when task demands align with technology capabilities, fit is high.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: The extent to which the technology meets my task needs
The technology has the right features for my tasks
The technology fits the way I work
notes: Central construct of TTF theory. Mediates the effect of task and technology on utilization and performance.

name: Utilization
definition: The extent to which the user actually uses the technology to perform their tasks. Behavioural use of the system; can be voluntary or mandated.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Frequency and extent of use
Use of specific features for task completion
notes: When fit is high, utilization is more likely; utilization can mediate the effect of fit on performance.

name: Performance Impact
definition: The effect of technology use on individual or organizational performance outcomes. The extent to which using the technology leads to better task completion, productivity, or effectiveness.
sourcePaper: Goodhue & Thompson (1995) - Task-Technology Fit and Individual Performance
measurementItems: Using the system improves my job performance
The system helps me accomplish tasks more quickly
The system has a positive impact on my work outcomes
notes: The key outcome in TTF: technology is more likely to improve performance when fit is high and utilization occurs.

name: Personal Innovativeness
definition: The willingness of an individual to try out any new information technology.
sourcePaper: Agarwal & Prasad (1998) - A Conceptual and Operational Definition of Personal Innovativeness in the Domain of Information Technology
measurementItems: If I heard about a new information technology, I would look for ways to experiment with it.
Among my peers, I am usually the first to try out new information technologies.
I like to experiment with new information technologies.
notes: Often used as a moderator or antecedent in technology acceptance; reflects trait-based openness to trying new IT.

name: Privacy Concern
definition: The degree to which individuals worry about misuse of their personal data.
sourcePaper: Various – widely used in privacy and information systems research
measurementItems: I am concerned that my personal information could be misused.
I worry about how my data is collected and used by online services.
I am concerned about the privacy of my personal information.
notes: Commonly used in research on digital platforms, social media, health apps, and fintech.

name: Perceived Vulnerability
definition: How likely someone believes they are to experience harm (e.g., data breaches).
sourcePaper: Risk perception and protection motivation literature
measurementItems: I believe I am likely to experience a data breach or security incident.
I feel vulnerable to online threats when using this technology.
I think my personal information is at risk when I use digital services.
notes: Often appears in cybersecurity and privacy research.

name: Trust
definition: The belief that a system or organization will behave reliably and ethically.
sourcePaper: Various – foundational in IS, marketing, and organizational behavior
measurementItems: I believe this system/organization is trustworthy.
I feel confident that my interests will be protected.
I can rely on this system/organization to act with integrity.
notes: One of the most widely studied constructs in digital adoption research.

name: Hedonic Motivation
definition: Using a system because it is fun or pleasurable, not purely functional.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh et al. (2012) - UTAUT2
measurementItems: Using the system is fun.
I find the system enjoyable to use.
Using the system provides me with a lot of enjoyment.
notes: Very common in technology acceptance research; key in UTAUT2 for consumer contexts.

name: Perceived Enjoyment
definition: The extent to which using a technology is enjoyable in itself.
sourcePaper: Davis et al. – extended TAM; consumer and IS research
measurementItems: I find using the system enjoyable.
Using the system is pleasurable.
I have fun using the system.
notes: Closely related to hedonic motivation; often used in adoption of entertainment or experiential technologies.

name: Self-Efficacy
definition: A person's belief in their ability to successfully perform a task.
sourcePaper: Bandura - Social cognitive theory
measurementItems: I am confident I can use the system to complete my tasks.
I have the skills necessary to use the system effectively.
I am able to use the system even if there is no one to help me.
notes: Originates from Bandura's social cognitive theory; in tech research often measured as "computer self-efficacy" or domain-specific self-efficacy.

name: User Involvement
definition: The degree to which users participate in the development and design of a product.
sourcePaper: Various – innovation and information systems research
measurementItems: Users were actively involved in the product development process.
User feedback significantly influenced product decisions.
We collaborated with users when defining product requirements.
notes: Central to research on product development, system design, participatory innovation; example areas: co-creation, participatory design, open innovation. Often tested: User involvement → product innovation performance.

name: Market Uncertainty
definition: The extent to which customer needs, preferences, and market demand are unpredictable.
sourcePaper: Innovation and strategic management literature
measurementItems: Customer needs are difficult to predict.
The market for this product changes rapidly.
It is difficult to forecast demand.
notes: Classic construct in innovation and strategy. Common sources: changing user needs, emerging markets, lack of market data. Typical relationships: Market uncertainty → need for experimentation; Market uncertainty → product development performance.

name: Innovation Performance
definition: The extent to which an organization successfully develops and introduces new products or innovations.
sourcePaper: Innovation management research
measurementItems: Our new products outperform competitors.
Our innovation projects achieve their objectives.
Our organization successfully introduces new products.
notes: Widely used in innovation management. Typical dimensions: success of new products, novelty of innovations, commercial performance. Relationships often studied: Organizational capabilities → innovation performance.

name: Organizational Learning
definition: The process through which organizations acquire, interpret, and apply knowledge to improve performance.
sourcePaper: Organizational theory and strategic management
measurementItems: Our organization learns from past development projects.
Lessons from previous projects improve future work.
Knowledge is systematically shared across teams.
notes: Key aspects: learning from experimentation, knowledge acquisition, knowledge sharing. Typical relationships: Experimentation → organizational learning; Organizational learning → innovation performance.

name: Experimentation Capability
definition: An organization's ability to test ideas, generate evidence, and learn through structured experimentation.
sourcePaper: Modern innovation and digital product research
measurementItems: Our organization systematically tests new ideas.
Experiments are conducted before full implementation.
We use prototypes to evaluate product concepts.
notes: Example activities: A/B testing, prototyping, pilot trials. Typical relationship: Experimentation capability → product innovation success.

name: Technology Adoption Intention
definition: The likelihood that an individual plans to use a specific technology.
sourcePaper: Various – UTAUT and adoption models
measurementItems: I intend to use this technology in the future.
I plan to use this system regularly.
I expect to continue using this technology.
notes: Appears in many adoption models including UTAUT. Typical relationship: Perceived usefulness → adoption intention.

name: Social Influence
definition: The process by which individuals or groups affect the beliefs, attitudes, intentions, or behaviors of others. In research it is often operationalized as perceived social influence or subjective norms and measured using scales or experiments.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh et al. (2003) - UTAUT; Ajzen (1991) - Theory of Planned Behavior; Festinger (1954) - Social Comparison Theory; Kelman (1958) - Compliance, Identification, and Internalization
theoreticalDomain: Social psychology, technology adoption, marketing and consumer behavior
measurementItems: People who are important to me think I should use this system.
People who influence my behavior think I should use this system.
Friends and colleagues encourage me to use this technology.
notes: Key construct in UTAUT (one of four core determinants of technology acceptance). In TPB operationalized as subjective norms. Explains how peers, superiors, or cultural norms shape adoption decisions. Used in health behavior (e.g. vaccine uptake, smoking cessation) and social media research (e.g. likes, shares, influencer endorsements). Bridges micro-level and macro-level analyses.

name: System Quality
definition: The quality of the technical performance of a system.
sourcePaper: DeLone & McLean IS success model; information systems research
measurementItems: The system is reliable.
The system responds quickly.
The system functions consistently.
notes: Dimensions often include: reliability, speed, usability. Typical relationship: System quality → user satisfaction.

name: Information Quality
definition: The quality of the information produced by a system.
sourcePaper: DeLone & McLean IS success model; information systems research
measurementItems: The system provides accurate information.
The information is relevant to my tasks.
The information provided is complete.
notes: Common dimensions: accuracy, relevance, completeness. Typical relationships: Information quality → system usefulness; Information quality → user satisfaction.

name: Attitude
definition: A person's overall evaluation of performing a behavior—favorable or unfavorable—based on beliefs about its outcomes and their evaluated importance (expectancy–value). In TRA/TPB, attitude toward the behavior is a key antecedent of behavioral intention.
sourcePaper: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211.
theoreticalDomain: Theory of Reasoned Action / Planned Behavior
measurementItems: For me, performing this behavior would be good/bad.
For me, performing this behavior would be harmful/beneficial.
For me, performing this behavior would be unpleasant/pleasant.
notes: Formed from salient beliefs about the behavior; central to predicting intention alongside subjective norms and perceived behavioral control (TPB).

name: Subjective Norms
definition: The perceived social pressure to perform or not perform a behavior—whether important referents believe one should or should not engage in the behavior.
sourcePaper: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211.
theoreticalDomain: Theory of Reasoned Action / Planned Behavior
measurementItems: Most people who are important to me would approve of my performing this behavior.
People whose opinions I value would want me to perform this behavior.
It is expected of me that I perform this behavior.
notes: In UTAUT, a related idea appears as social influence. In TPB, subjective norm is one of three direct predictors of intention.

name: Perceived Behavioral Control
definition: The perceived ease or difficulty of performing a behavior, reflecting beliefs about control over internal and external factors that may facilitate or impede performance. Often linked to self-efficacy and controllability.
sourcePaper: Ajzen, I. (1991). The theory of planned behavior. Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes, 50(2), 179–211.
measurementItems: For me, performing this behavior would be easy/difficult.
Whether I perform this behavior is entirely up to me.
I have the resources and opportunities needed to perform this behavior.
notes: Extends TRA to TPB; together with intention can predict behavior when volitional control is realistic. Distinct from but related to Bandura’s self-efficacy.

name: Facilitating Conditions
definition: The degree to which an individual believes that organizational and technical infrastructure exists to support use of the system. It captures enabling conditions and resources in the environment—such as device availability, connectivity, help-desk support, training, and compatibility with existing systems—rather than beliefs about the technology alone.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh, V., Morris, M. G., Davis, G. B., & Davis, F. D. (2003). User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view. MIS Quarterly, 27(3), 425–478.
measurementItems: I have the resources necessary to use the system.
A specific person (or group) is available for assistance with system difficulties.
I have the knowledge required to use the system.
The system is compatible with other systems I use.
measurementScale: Likert scale (adapted from Venkatesh et al., 2003)
notes: Type: enabling conditions / resources. Level: environment or context. In UTAUT, facilitating conditions (and sometimes intention) predict use behavior. Includes availability of resources, technical support, training, and compatibility with existing systems.

name: Voluntariness
definition: The degree to which use of a system is perceived as voluntary rather than mandated—reflecting freedom of choice and the absence of coercion or compulsory requirement to adopt.
sourcePaper: Venkatesh, V., Morris, M. G., Davis, G. B., & Davis, F. D. (2003). User acceptance of information technology: Toward a unified view. MIS Quarterly, 27(3), 425–478.
measurementItems: My use of the system is voluntary.
My manager does not require me to use the system.
Although it might be helpful, using the system is certainly not compulsory in my job.
measurementScale: Likert scale (context-dependent; stronger in mandatory settings)
notes: Type: contextual perception. Level: situational (mandatory vs voluntary adoption). Role in UTAUT: often modeled as a moderator—e.g. strengthening or weakening effects of social influence and expectancy constructs on intention when use is more or less voluntary.
